rules: Explain why we ask in plain language

eq: I do not currently have periods

screens.title: A quick note
screens.body: If you've missed your period for 3 months or longer and you're not pregnant, it can be helpful to check in with a clinician—especially if this is new for you. We'll keep going, and we'll use your answers to provide context (not diagnoses).

intro.title: Let's start with a few basics
intro.body: These questions help us understand your current life stage and set a baseline. Your answers help the Arc connect signals across systems (like hormones, sleep, and metabolism).

items.question: How old are you?
items.support: Age helps us interpret life-stage patterns in hormones, metabolism, and long-term monitoring (like bone and cardiovascular health).

items.question: Which life stage best describes you right now?
items.support: Life stage strongly influences hormone patterns, sleep, and energy regulation.

items.question: What is your current height?
items.support: This helps us interpret weight changes and health signals more accurately.

items.question: What is your current weight?
items.support: Weight is optional context that helps interpret metabolism and energy signals over time.

items.question: Has your weight changed in the last 3 months?
items.support: Recent changes can reflect shifts in metabolism, stress load, sleep, or hormonal rhythm.

items.title: Your body works as a system
items.body: Energy, hormones, sleep, and stress influence each other. Next we'll look at cycle signals that often add important context.

intro.title: Cycle signals add context
intro.body: Many patterns—like sleep changes, cravings, and recovery—shift across the cycle. These questions help us interpret those shifts calmly and clearly.

items.question: Are your menstrual cycles usually regular?
options: I do not currently have periods
items.support: Cycle regularity helps us understand hormonal rhythm stability.

items.question: About how many days is your typical cycle?
items.support: Cycle length helps us interpret timing-based patterns (like energy dips or sleep changes).

items.question: How heavy are your periods usually?
items.support: Heavier bleeding can affect iron stores, which may influence fatigue and recovery.

items.question: Do you pass large blood clots during your period?
items.support: This helps us understand bleeding patterns that can relate to iron and energy signals.

items.question: How painful are your periods usually?
items.support: Pain patterns can influence stress signals, sleep, and recovery.

items.question: How strong are your PMS symptoms overall?
items.support: PMS intensity can help us interpret mood, sleep, and energy changes across the month.

items.question: Have you missed your period for 3 months or longer (and you're not pregnant)?
items.support: This helps us interpret hormonal rhythm signals and know when extra context may be helpful.

items.question: What type of contraception do you currently use?
items.support: Hormonal contraception can change cycle-related signals and how we interpret patterns.

items.title: Next: energy & metabolism signals
items.body: Fatigue, cravings, and exercise tolerance can reflect sleep, stress load, or metabolic regulation. We'll ask a few questions to understand your pattern.

intro.title: Energy isn't one signal
intro.body: Energy can be influenced by sleep quality, stress load, blood sugar stability, iron status, and cycle timing. We'll focus on what you feel day-to-day.

items.question: How often do you feel unusually tired or fatigued?
items.support: Frequency helps us understand whether this is occasional or a consistent pattern.

items.question: When you feel fatigued, how intense is it?
items.support: Intensity helps us weight this signal when building your system map.

items.question: When is your fatigue usually worst?
items.support: Timing can help distinguish sleep-driven vs. metabolic-driven patterns.

items.question: Do you experience sudden energy crashes during the day?
items.support: Energy crashes can be linked to stress load, sleep debt, or blood sugar variability.

items.question: Do these crashes tend to happen after meals?
items.support: Post-meal crashes can be a clue about blood sugar stability.

items.question: How strong are sugar cravings for you lately?
items.support: Cravings can shift with cycle phase, stress hormones, and blood sugar regulation.

items.question: How well do you tolerate exercise right now?
items.support: Exercise tolerance can reflect recovery capacity, sleep, stress load, and energy availability.

items.title: Next: mood & cognitive signals
items.body: Mood, anxiety, and brain fog often reflect how stress, sleep, and hormones interact. We'll keep this simple.

items.question: How often do you feel anxious or on edge?
items.support: This helps us understand stress response load over time.

items.question: When anxiety shows up, how intense does it feel?
items.support: Intensity helps us weight this signal in your system map.

items.question: Do these feelings tend to worsen before your period?
items.support: Cycle-linked mood changes can help us interpret hormonal rhythm and stress interactions.

items.question: How often do you experience brain fog or difficulty concentrating?
items.support: Brain fog patterns can reflect sleep quality, stress load, and energy regulation.

items.question: How intense is the brain fog when it happens?
items.support: This helps us weight cognitive signals in your overall pattern.

items.title: Next: sleep signals
items.body: Sleep is one of the body's main regulatory systems. Even small changes can affect mood, recovery, and metabolism.

items.question: On average, how many hours do you sleep per night?
items.support: Duration helps us interpret fatigue, recovery, and stress resilience.

items.question: How would you rate your sleep quality lately?
items.support: Quality often matters as much as quantity for recovery.

items.question: How long does it usually take you to fall asleep?
items.support: Longer time to fall asleep can reflect stress load, nervous system activation, or schedule mismatch.

items.question: How often do you wake during the night?
items.support: Night waking can influence recovery and next-day energy.

items.question: Do you often wake around 3–4 AM?
items.support: This can be a helpful pattern marker for sleep architecture and stress rhythm (not a diagnosis).

items.question: How often do you wake up feeling unrefreshed?
items.support: Unrefreshed sleep helps us interpret recovery capacity even when sleep duration looks fine.

items.title: Next: gut & digestion
items.body: Digestion can reflect stress, food tolerance, and hormonal shifts. We'll ask a few quick questions.

items.question: How often do you experience bloating?
items.support: Bloating patterns can be influenced by food tolerance, stress, and cycle timing.

items.question: How often do you experience constipation?
items.support: Constipation patterns can relate to hydration, stress, hormones, and gut motility.

items.question: How often do you experience diarrhea or loose stools?
items.support: This helps us understand gut variability and sensitivity patterns.

items.question: Do certain foods frequently trigger digestive symptoms for you?
items.support: Food-triggered symptoms can help us identify sensitivity patterns worth monitoring.

items.title: Next: skin & hair signals
items.body: Skin, hair, and nails can reflect hormonal shifts, stress load, and micronutrient reserves. We'll keep it quick.

items.question: Have you noticed increased hair shedding or thinning?
items.support: Hair changes can relate to stress load, hormone shifts, iron status, and thyroid context.

items.question: How severe are acne or skin breakouts for you lately?
items.support: Breakouts can shift with cycle phase, stress, and androgen-related patterns.

items.question: How dry does your skin feel lately?
items.support: Dryness can reflect hydration, environment, and sometimes thyroid or hormonal context.

items.question: Do your nails break easily?
items.support: Nails can reflect micronutrient reserves and overall recovery state.

items.title: Next: lifestyle & stress
items.body: Lifestyle context helps us interpret signals accurately—especially when stress, sleep, and training change.

items.question: How would you rate your current stress level?
items.support: Stress level helps us interpret sleep quality, recovery, cravings, and mood patterns.

items.question: Have you experienced a major life stressor in the past 3 months?
items.support: Recent changes can shift patterns quickly—this helps us avoid over-interpreting short-term fluctuations.

items.question: How many days per week do you exercise (on average)?
items.support: Training load is important context for recovery and fatigue signals.

items.question: How many cups of caffeine do you have per day (on average)?
items.support: Caffeine can influence sleep latency, anxiety, and perceived energy.

items.question: How often do you drink alcohol?
items.support: Alcohol can affect sleep architecture and recovery signals.

items.title: Next: medical history
items.body: A few health history questions help us avoid false pattern matches and interpret signals responsibly.

items.question: Have you ever been diagnosed with PCOS?
items.support: This helps us interpret cycle and androgen-related patterns accurately.

items.question: Have you ever been diagnosed with a thyroid condition?
items.support: Thyroid context can influence energy, skin/hair, and metabolism signals.

items.question: Have you been diagnosed with endometriosis?
items.support: This can add context to pain patterns and cycle-related symptoms.

items.question: Have you been diagnosed with an autoimmune condition?
items.support: Autoimmune history can affect fatigue, inflammation signals, and recovery patterns.

items.title: Optional: lab values
items.body: If you have recent lab results, you can add them now. If not, you can skip—your dashboard will still work.

intro.title: Add lab values if you have them
intro.body: Labs can clarify certain patterns—especially fatigue, hair changes, and metabolic variability. You can leave any field blank.

items.question: Ferritin (iron stores) — if you know it
items.support: Ferritin reflects iron storage and can influence fatigue and recovery.

items.question: TSH (thyroid) — if you know it
items.support: TSH is commonly used to understand thyroid context related to energy and metabolism.

items.question: Vitamin D — if you know it
items.support: Vitamin D can relate to mood, immune function, and long-term bone health context.

items.question: Fasting glucose — if you know it
items.support: Fasting glucose helps interpret blood sugar stability patterns.

items.question: HbA1c — if you know it
items.support: HbA1c reflects average blood sugar levels over time and can add useful context.

items.question: Fasting insulin — if you know it
items.support: Insulin can help clarify energy crashes and craving patterns for some people.

items.title: Last: family risk
items.body: Family history helps us personalize long-term monitoring without guessing. This isn't about predicting—just adding context.

items.question: Does diabetes run in your family?
items.support: This can influence what we recommend monitoring over time (like blood sugar stability markers).

items.question: Is there a family history of early cardiovascular disease?
items.support: This helps personalize longer-term cardiovascular awareness in a calm, non-alarmist way.

items.question: Is there a family history of breast cancer?
items.support: This is used only for preventive context and education—not risk prediction.

items.question: Is there a family history of osteoporosis?
items.support: This helps personalize bone health awareness over time, especially across life stages.

outro.title: You're done
outro.body: We'll use your answers to generate your biological lens, system map, and what to watch—based on patterns, not diagnoses.
